primer:
## Inheritance
**Inheritance** lets a derived class extend a base, reusing and adding to its members: `class Dog : public Animal` gives `Dog` everything `public`/`protected` in the base. **`public` inheritance models 'is-a'** (a Derived is usable wherever a Base is expected). **`private` inheritance** hides the base interface and models 'implemented-in-terms-of' — usually better done with composition (a member).

## Virtual functions & polymorphism
A **virtual function** is declared `virtual` in the base, so a call through a base **pointer or reference** dispatches to the most-derived override at **runtime** (dynamic dispatch). Without `virtual`, the call resolves statically by the pointer's static type. **Runtime polymorphism** = treating different derived types uniformly through one base interface, with behaviour chosen at runtime (a `std::vector<Shape*>` each `->area()` dispatches correctly).

A **pure virtual** `virtual void f() = 0;` has no required body and must be overridden; a class with at least one pure virtual is **abstract** (cannot be instantiated). An **interface** in C++ = an abstract base of only pure virtuals plus a virtual destructor.

Under the hood, a polymorphic object stores a hidden **vptr** to its class's **vtable** (table of function pointers); a virtual call reads the vptr, looks up the function, and jumps there — one indirection per call.

## Keywords
**`override`** marks an intended override so the compiler verifies a matching base virtual exists (catching signature typos). **`final`** forbids further overriding (on a function) or inheritance (on a class), and can enable devirtualisation.

Don't confuse **overriding** (redefining an inherited virtual with the *same* signature — runtime dispatch) with **overloading** (same name, *different* parameters in one scope — compile-time resolution).

## Pitfalls
A polymorphic base needs a **virtual destructor** (`virtual ~Base() = default;`): `delete`ing a derived object through a base pointer with a non-virtual base destructor runs only `~Base`, never the derived destructor — UB and usually a leak. **Object slicing**: copying a derived object into a base **by value** (`Base b = derivedObj;`) keeps only the base subobject and drops derived data/overrides — handle polymorphic objects through pointers/references, never by value. (`std::unique_ptr<Shape>` in a `std::vector` gives correct dispatch and automatic cleanup.)
name: C++ — Inheritance & polymorphism
description: Inheritance, virtual functions and runtime polymorphism, abstract classes and interfaces, the importance of virtual destructors, override/final, and slicing. The dynamic-dispatch half of C++ OOP.

cards.definition: A mechanism where a derived class extends a base class, reusing and adding to its members. `class Dog : public Animal` gives `Dog` everything `public`/`protected` in the base plus its own additions. `public` inheritance models an 'is-a' relationship.
cards.summary: A derived class extends a base, modelling 'is-a'.

cards.definition: A member function declared `virtual` in the base class, so a call through a base pointer or reference dispatches to the most-derived override at runtime (dynamic dispatch). Without `virtual`, the call is resolved statically by the static type of the pointer.
cards.summary: Resolved at runtime to the derived override (dynamic dispatch).

cards.definition: Treating different derived types uniformly through a common base interface, with the correct behaviour selected at runtime via virtual functions. A `std::vector<Shape*>` of mixed shapes can each `->area()` and dispatch to the right `area` override.
cards.summary: One base interface, behaviour chosen at runtime per object.

cards.term: What is a pure virtual function and an abstract class?
cards.definition: A pure virtual function `virtual void f() = 0;` has no (required) implementation and must be overridden by derived classes. A class with at least one pure virtual is abstract — it cannot be instantiated, only used as a base or interface.
cards.summary: `= 0` makes it pure virtual; a class with one is abstract.

cards.term: A base class meant for polymorphic use should have a virtual destructor.
cards.summary: True — otherwise deleting via a base pointer skips the derived destructor (UB).
cards.definition: True. If you `delete` a derived object through a base pointer and the base destructor is not `virtual`, only the base part is destroyed — the derived destructor never runs (undefined behaviour, often a leak). Polymorphic base classes need a `virtual ~Base() = default;`.

It declares that a member function is intended to override a base virtual. The compiler then checks that a matching base virtual exists — catching typos or signature mismatches that would silently create a new function instead of overriding. Always use it on intended overrides.
cards.summary: Marks an intended override so the compiler verifies it.

cards.term: What does `final` mean on a class or virtual function?
cards.definition: `final` forbids further overriding or derivation: a `final` virtual function cannot be overridden again, and a `final` class cannot be inherited from. It documents intent and can enable compiler devirtualisation optimisations.

cards.sub: A) copying a derived object into a base-by-value variable drops the derived parts    B) cutting a vector in half    C) a way to speed up copies    D) calling a base method from a derived one
cards.definition: Correct: A) copying a derived object into a base-by-value variable drops the derived parts. `Base b = derivedObj;` copies only the base subobject — the derived data and overrides are 'sliced' off. Avoid by handling polymorphic objects through pointers or references, never by value.

cards.term: Polymorphism requires accessing the object through a base {{pointer}} or reference; calling a virtual on an object by value resolves statically.
cards.summary: Dynamic dispatch needs a base pointer/reference, not a by-value object.
cards.definition: Virtual dispatch happens only through a pointer or reference to the base, because that is where the runtime type can differ from the static type. A by-value `Base` is exactly a `Base`, so its virtuals resolve statically and slicing has already occurred.

cards.term: How does a virtual call work under the hood (vtable)?
cards.definition: A polymorphic object stores a hidden pointer (vptr) to its class's table of function pointers (the vtable). A virtual call reads the vptr, looks up the function in the vtable, and jumps there — so the implementation matches the object's actual type. This adds one indirection per virtual call.
cards.summary: vptr → vtable lookup selects the right override at runtime.

cards.term: How do you model an interface in C++?
cards.definition: Define an abstract base class containing only pure virtual functions (and a virtual destructor). Derived classes implement them. This gives an interface contract without multiple-inheritance complications: `class Drawable { public: virtual void draw() const = 0; virtual ~Drawable() = default; };`.
cards.summary: An abstract base of pure virtuals = an interface.

cards.term: `private` inheritance models an 'is-a' relationship just like `public` inheritance.
cards.summary: False — public inheritance is 'is-a'; private is 'implemented-in-terms-of'.
cards.definition: False. `public` inheritance expresses 'is-a' (a Derived can be used wherever a Base is expected). `private` inheritance hides the base interface and expresses 'implemented-in-terms-of' — usually better modelled by composition (a member).

cards.term: What is the difference between overriding and overloading?
cards.sub: A) overriding replaces a base virtual with the same signature; overloading is same name, different parameters in one scope    B) they are synonyms    C) overloading happens at runtime, overriding at compile time    D) overriding requires different parameter types
cards.definition: Correct: A) overriding replaces a base virtual with the same signature; overloading is same name, different parameters in one scope. Override = redefining an inherited virtual (runtime dispatch, identical signature). Overload = multiple functions sharing a name distinguished by parameters (compile-time resolution).

cards.term:
Exercise: this leaks the derived part — fix it.
`struct Base { ~Base(){} virtual void f(){} }; struct D : Base { int* big = new int[1000]; ~D(){ delete[] big; } }; Base* p = new D; delete p;`
cards.sub: What does delete p call when ~Base is non-virtual?
cards.definition: `~Base` is not `virtual`, so `delete p` (a `Base*`) calls only `~Base`, never `~D` — the `big` array leaks and behaviour is undefined. Make the base destructor virtual: `virtual ~Base() = default;`. Then `delete p` dispatches to `~D` first.
cards.summary: Non-virtual base dtor skips ~D → leak; make ~Base virtual.

cards.term: Exercise: design a tiny shape hierarchy where `area()` is polymorphic. Sketch the base.
cards.definition: `struct Shape { virtual double area() const = 0; virtual ~Shape() = default; };` makes `Shape` an abstract interface. `struct Circle : Shape { double r; double area() const override { return 3.14159 * r * r; } };`. Now a `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Shape>>` can hold mixed shapes and call `->area()` with correct dispatch and automatic cleanup.
cards.summary: Abstract Shape with pure-virtual area() + virtual dtor; derived override.
